title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community book festival. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Julia, The Programme Scheduler
Julia first volunteered for the book festival because she loved the idea of writers appearing in ordinary community spaces rather than formal city theatres. Her work, however, was less romantic than she expected. She spent evenings checking spreadsheets of rooms, chairs, microphones and author arrival times. [47]Three days before the festival, she discovered that two visiting writers had been booked into the same small hall at exactly the same hour.[/47] Moving one event meant rewriting posters and apologising to a school group, but it prevented chaos. [48]Her experience as a project administrator helped her build a colour-coded timetable that showed every room change and technical requirement.[/48] Julia learned that audiences rarely see the planning unless something goes wrong. Still, she enjoyed watching volunteers relax when they knew where to go. By Sunday afternoon, her timetable was covered in coffee marks, but every session had started within five minutes of schedule.
texts.B:
### Mateo, The Bilingual Session Helper
Mateo happily helped at bilingual poetry sessions after a local poet he admired asked whether he could support Spanish-speaking visitors. [49]That personal request from the poet persuaded him to volunteer, although he had never interpreted at a public event before.[/49] He assumed his language skills would be enough, but live literature created surprises. [50]He found that translating jokes and wordplay immediately was much harder than translating practical information at the welcome desk.[/50] When he could not reproduce a pun, he explained the idea instead of pretending nothing had happened. Mateo also helped visitors ask questions without feeling self-conscious about their accents. He was touched when one elderly man said he had understood a poem his granddaughter had recommended. The work left Mateo exhausted, because he had to listen intensely for whole sessions. He now believes translation at cultural events is not a side service, but part of the performance itself.
texts.C:
### Poppy, The Children’s Tent Leader
Poppy ran lively activities in the children’s tent, which sounded cheerful until twenty-five excited children arrived at once. She had planned a bookmark-making craft using glitter glue and tiny beads. [51]After the first group left sticky handprints across three tables and a borrowed rug, she replaced the craft with folded paper animals and washable pencils.[/51] The tent instantly became easier to reset between sessions. Poppy quickly discovered that children listened better when instructions were acted out rather than explained from the front of the tent. She also kept spare picture books for children who needed a quieter moment. [52]Her favourite memory was a shy girl reading one full page aloud to her younger brother while their mother stood outside the tent crying quietly.[/52] Poppy says children’s events should not feel like childcare attached to the real festival. For many families, they are the first doorway into books, stories and libraries.
texts.D:
### Samira, The Quiet Room Coordinator
Samira offered to coordinate the festival’s quiet room because crowded events had often overwhelmed her when she was younger and attending similar fairs. [53]Her own experience of needing somewhere calm made the role feel important to her.[/53] The room contained low lights, cushions, ear defenders and a table where visitors could sit without talking to anyone. [54]Her hardest task was protecting the quiet atmosphere while a lively storytelling performance took place on the other side of a thin partition.[/54] She asked stewards to guide noisy queues away from the door and moved the snack table further down the corridor. [55]She also designed simple signs using symbols rather than long explanations, so tired visitors could find the room quickly.[/55] Some people stayed only two minutes; others missed whole events. [56]At the end of the weekend, Samira wrote notes on layout, lighting and crowd flow so next year’s coordinator would not start from memory.[/56]

questions.question: found a serious timetable clash shortly before the event began?
questions.explanation: Julia discovered that two writers were booked into the same hall at the same time.

questions.question: used administrative experience to create a detailed visual schedule?
questions.explanation: Julia used project administration experience to build a colour-coded timetable.

questions.question: agreed to volunteer after being personally asked by someone they admired?
questions.explanation: Mateo volunteered after a local poet he admired asked for help.

questions.question: discovered that humour was especially difficult to translate instantly?
questions.explanation: Mateo found jokes and wordplay much harder to translate immediately.

questions.question: changed an activity after the first version caused a messy practical problem?
questions.explanation: Poppy replaced the glitter-glue craft after it left sticky handprints.

questions.question: describes a young person reading aloud and causing a parent to become emotional?
questions.explanation: A shy girl read to her brother while her mother cried quietly.

questions.question: chose their role because of their own past need for a calm space?
questions.explanation: Samira knew from personal experience how important a quiet room could be.

questions.question: had to preserve a calm environment despite noise from a nearby activity?
questions.explanation: Samira protected the quiet room while storytelling happened beyond a thin partition.

questions.question: made symbol-based signs so people could locate a space without reading much text?
questions.explanation: Samira designed simple signs using symbols rather than long explanations.

questions.question: recorded details about the room setup for the next person in charge?
questions.explanation: Samira wrote notes on layout, lighting and crowd flow for next year’s coordinator.
